title: Numbers & Time
shortSummary: Count, tell the time, and talk about days in Filipino, mixing native and borrowed numbers as locals do.
culturalNote: Everyday Filipino blends two number systems. Native Tagalog numbers (isa, dalawa, tatlo) are used for counting things and for ages, but when telling time and counting money many Filipinos switch to Spanish-derived numbers (alas-dos, singko, diyes). At the market a vendor may quote a price in Spanish numbers, so it pays to recognise both. Time itself is famously flexible: Filipino time often means people arrive a little late to casual gatherings, though work, church, and formal appointments are taken seriously. Asking Anong oras na? (What time is it?) is part of daily small talk, and the days of the week (Lunes to Linggo) also come from Spanish.

grammar.explanation: Filipino uses native numbers (isa, dalawa, tatlo) for counting objects and ages, but commonly uses Spanish-derived numbers (uno, dos, tres; singko, diyes) for time and money. Both are correct; the context tells you which to choose.
grammar.commonMistake: Using native numbers for clock time, such as saying alas-tatlo instead of alas-tres.

grammar.explanation: To say the hour, use alas plus the Spanish number: alas-dos (two), alas-tres (three). One o'clock is the exception, ala-una. Add na to mean 'it is now'.
grammar.commonMistake: Saying alas-una instead of the correct ala-una for one o'clock.

dialogue.setup: Nena buys mangoes at the palengke (wet market) and checks the time before her bus.

lines.speaker: Nena
lines.native: Sige po, bibili ako ng anim. Magkano lahat?
lines.english: Okay, I'll buy six. How much in total?

lines.speaker: Nena
lines.native: Heto po. Pala, anong oras na po?
lines.english: Here you go. By the way, what time is it?

lines.speaker: Tindera
lines.native: Alas-dos na po. May byahe ka ba?
lines.english: It is two o'clock. Do you have a trip?

lines.speaker: Nena
lines.native: Opo, alas-tres ang bus ko. Salamat po, ingat!
lines.english: Yes, my bus is at three. Thank you, take care!

quiz.question: How do you ask 'What time is it?' in Filipino?
quiz.explanation: Anong oras na? is the everyday way to ask the time.

quiz.question: Which is the correct way to say 'one o'clock'?
quiz.explanation: One o'clock is the exception: ala-una, not alas-una.

quiz.question: Native numbers: isa, dalawa, _____, apat (the word for 'three').
quiz.explanation: The native word for three is tatlo.

quiz.question: Which number system do Filipinos usually use for telling clock time?
quiz.explanation: Time is usually told with Spanish-derived numbers like alas-dos.

quiz.question: Arrange: ito / magkano
quiz.explanation: Magkano ito? means 'How much is this?'
